input_title: Create a work breakdown structure | Microsoft Learn
page_title: Create a work breakdown structure | Microsoft Learn
meta_description: This article explains how to create a work breakdown structure (WBS) inclusive of the basic controls in the new scheduling interface.

sections.heading: Share via

blocks.text: Access to this page requires authorization. You can try signing in or changing directories .
Access to this page requires authorization. You can try changing directories .

sections.summary: You are being denied access to the page due to authorization issues. To resolve this, try signing in to the system or platform. If signing in doesn't work, attempt to change directories to see if access is granted.

sections.heading: In this article

blocks.text: A project schedule communicates what work must be completed, which resources do the work, and the timeframe in which the work must be completed. The schedule reflects all the work associated with delivering the project on time. In Dynamics 365 Project Operations, you create a project schedule by:

items: Breaking down the work into manageable tasks.
Estimating the time that is required to do each task.
Setting task dependencies.
Setting task durations.
Estimating the generic resources that complete the tasks.

blocks.text: You create the project schedule on the Schedule tab on the Project page.

sections.summary: To create a project schedule in Dynamics 365 Project Operations, break down the work into manageable tasks. Estimate the time required for each task and set task dependencies and durations. Identify the generic resources needed to complete each task. The project schedule is created on the Schedule tab of the Project page, reflecting all work required to deliver the project on time.

sections.heading: Tasks

blocks.text: The first step in creating a project schedule is to break down the work into manageable portions. The schedule in Project Operations supports the following features:

items: Summary or container tasks
Leaf node tasks

sections.summary: To create a project schedule, start by breaking down the work into smaller, manageable tasks. The project schedule supports two main types of tasks: summary tasks, which group smaller tasks together, and leaf node tasks, which are individual tasks. Breaking down work into these tasks helps create a clear and organized project schedule.

sections.heading: Summary tasks

blocks.text: Summary tasks can store other summary tasks or leaf node tasks. They have no work effort or cost of their own. Instead, their work effort and cost roll up from their container tasks. The start date of the summary task is the start date of the container tasks, and the end date is the end date of the container tasks. You can edit the name of a summary task, but you can't edit scheduling properties, including effort, dates, and duration. If you delete a summary task, you also delete all its container tasks.

sections.summary:
Here's a summary of summary tasks in 4 clear sentences:

Summary tasks are containers that hold other summary tasks or individual tasks, but don't have their own work effort or cost. Their work effort and cost are calculated from the tasks they contain. You can edit a summary task's name, but not its scheduling properties like dates or duration. Deleting a summary task will also delete all the tasks it contains.

sections.heading: Leaf node tasks

blocks.text: Leaf node tasks represent the most granular work on the project. They have an estimated effort, resources, planned start and end dates, and a duration.

sections.summary: Leaf node tasks are the smallest units of work in a project. They have specific details, including estimated effort, required resources, and planned start and end dates. Each task also has a defined duration, outlining how long it's expected to take. These details help project managers plan and track progress effectively.

sections.heading: Add a task

blocks.text: Complete the following steps to add one or more tasks.

items: Go to Projects and select the project record you want to open.
Select the Tasks tab.
Select Add new task , enter a name for the task, and then press Enter.
Enter another task name and press Enter again until you have a full list of tasks.

sections.summary: To add a task, go to the Projects section and open the desired project record. Click on the Tasks tab and select "Add new task." Enter the task name and press Enter to save it. Continue adding tasks by entering their names and pressing Enter until your list is complete.

sections.heading: Manage hierarchy of a task

blocks.text: When you indent a task, it becomes a child of the task that is directly above it. The schedule ID of the task is then recalculated so that it's based on the schedule ID of its new parent and follows the outline numbering scheme. The parent task becomes a summary task and a rollup of its child tasks. When you promote a task, it isn't a child of the task that was its parent. The schedule ID is recalculated so that it reflects the task's updated depth and position in the hierarchy. The effort, cost, and dates of the previous parent task are recalculated so that they don't include this task.
Complete the following steps to indent or promote a task.

items: On the Project page, on the Tasks tab, under Summary tasks , select the three vertical dots by the task name, and then select Make subtask .
Select the task to indent or promote. To select more than one task, select a task, select and hold the Ctrl key, and then select more tasks.
Select Indent or Promote subtask to move tasks under or out from under summary tasks.

sections.summary: To manage a task hierarchy, indenting a task makes it a child of the task above it, recalculating its schedule ID and making the parent a summary task. Promoting a task removes it from its parent, updating its schedule ID and recalculating the parent's effort, cost, and dates. To indent or promote a task, go to the Project page, Tasks tab, and select the three vertical dots next to the task name. From there, choose "Make subtask" and then select "Indent" or "Promote subtask" to move the task under or out from under a summary task. This can be done for multiple tasks by holding the Ctrl key while selecting.

sections.heading: Move tasks up and down

blocks.text: You can move tasks to any level in the work breakdown structure in one of two ways:

items: Select one or more tasks, and drag them to the desired location.
Select one or more tasks, right-click and select Cut , select the destination cell in the schedule, and then right-click and select Paste .

sections.summary: To move tasks in the work breakdown structure, you have two options. You can drag and drop one or more selected tasks to the desired location. Alternatively, you can right-click on the tasks, select "Cut", then right-click on the destination cell and select "Paste". This allows you to easily reorganize tasks to any level in the structure.

sections.heading: Task attributes

blocks.text: A task's name describes the work that must be completed. In Project Operations, the attributes associated with a task describe the schedule of the task and its staffing requirements.

sections.summary: In Project Operations, a task's attributes provide essential details about the task. The task name describes the work to be done. Task attributes also outline the task's schedule and staffing requirements, helping to organize and plan the project effectively.

sections.heading: Schedule attributes

blocks.text: The Effort , Start date , End date , and Duration attributes define the schedule for the task.
The following table shows more schedule attributes.

rows: Completed work for the task in hours.
Displays the duration in days for the task.
Total work for the task in hours.
The percentage of the task that's complete.
The task board can be grouped by bucket. Therefore, each bucket has its own column.
The remaining work for the task in hours.
The ID of the task in the work breakdown structure.

blocks.text: As an administrator, you can define custom fields on the task entity. However, you can't display these fields on the schedule grid. To see your custom fields, add them to the Project Task details page.

sections.summary:
Here's a summary of the section on schedule attributes:

Key schedule attributes for tasks include Effort, Start date, End date, and Duration. Additional attributes provide more details, such as Effort Completed, Total Effort, Finish date and time, and % Complete. Administrators can create custom fields for tasks, but these fields can only be viewed on the Project Task details page, not on the schedule grid.

sections.heading: Staffing attributes

blocks.text: Access staffing attributes through the Resources field in the schedule. You can search for an existing resource, or select Create and, in the Quick Create pane, add a project team member as a new resource. When you search for a resource by using the resource picker in the task grid, board view, or Gantt, the search returns either existing project team members or active bookable resources.

Use the Role , Resourcing Unit , and Position Name fields to describe the staffing requirements for the task. These staffing attributes, together with the task schedule, help you find available resources to do this task.

items: Role â Specify the type of resource that is required to do the task.

Resourcing unit â Specify the unit that resources for the task should be assigned from. This attribute affects the cost and sales estimate for the task if the cost and bill rate for the resource are set based on resourcing units.
Position name â Enter a name for the generic resource that serves as a placeholder for the resource that ultimately completes the work.

blocks.text: The Resources field holds the position name of the generic resource or named resource when you find one.

The Category field holds the values that indicate a broader type of work that the task can be grouped into. This field doesn't affect scheduling or staffing. Instead, use the field only for reporting.

sections.summary: To access staffing attributes, go to the Resources field in the schedule and search for existing resources or create a new one. Use the Role, Resourcing Unit, and Position Name fields to describe task staffing requirements, which help find available resources. The Role field specifies the resource type needed, Resourcing Unit affects cost and sales estimates, and Position Name is a placeholder for the resource that completes the work. These attributes, along with the task schedule, aid in resource allocation.

sections.heading: Task dependencies

blocks.text: You can use the schedule in Project Operations to create predecessor relationships between tasks. The Predecessor field uses one or more values to indicate the tasks that a task depends on. When you assign predecessor values to a task, the task can start only after you complete all of the predecessor tasks. Because of the dependency, the planned start date of the task is reset to the date when the predecessor tasks are completed.
Project Operations supports four dependency types.

rows: As the default dependency, Task B (successor) can start only after the finish of task A (predecessor)
Task B (successor) can start only with or after the start of task A (predecessor)
Task B (successor) can finish only after the finish of task A (predecessor)
Task B (successor) can finish only after the start task A (predecessor)

blocks.text: For precise planning, Project Operations provides the concept of lead and lag to the project manager.

rows: Task B (successor) can start immediately after the Task A (predecessor) finishes
Task B (successor) can start only after the specified lag duration has elapsed after Task A (predecessor) finishes (positive offset)
Task B (successor) can start earlier by the defined lead time prior to the completion of Task A (predecessor) (negative offset)

blocks.text: The task mode has no effect on updates that you make to the start and end dates of predecessor or dependent tasks.

sections.summary: In Project Operations, you can create predecessor relationships between tasks to establish dependencies. There are four dependency types: Finish-to-start, Start-to-start, Finish-to-Finish, and Start-to-Finish, which determine when a task can start or finish based on its predecessor. Additionally, you can use lead and lag to fine-tune task timing, allowing for immediate starts, delayed starts (lag), or early starts (lead) after a predecessor task is completed. This helps you plan tasks more precisely and ensures that dependent tasks are updated accordingly.

sections.heading: Task labels

blocks.text: You can apply labels on tasks and use this capability to quickly see what they have in common, to sort or filter. For more information, see Use labels to sort tasks in Microsoft Project for the web .

sections.summary: Task labels allow you to categorize and organize tasks in a way that makes sense for your project. By applying labels, you can quickly identify commonalities between tasks and group them accordingly. Labels can also be used to sort and filter tasks, making it easier to manage and prioritize your work. This feature helps streamline your workflow and improve project visibility.

sections.heading: Task history

blocks.text: Task history records all the changes you make to a task. For each edit, the history shows details such as who made the change, when they made it, what property they changed, the previous value, and the new value.
The history records changes that include edits such as:

items: Adding or removing labels
Changing the duration or effort
Editing checklists
Adding or removing attachments
Edits to any custom columns

sections.summary: Task history keeps a record of all changes made to a task, including who made the change, when, and what was changed. This record includes edits such as adding/removing labels, changing duration or effort, and modifying custom columns. The history also tracks changes to checklists and attachments. This allows for easy tracking of task updates and changes over time.

sections.heading: Understanding the impacts of duration, resource calendars, and project calendars on tasks

blocks.text: A task's duration is the number of working hours between the start time of the start date and the end time of the finish date of the task. By default, Project for the Web defines duration units of measure as follows.

You can edit these duration conversions at the project level to support users in regions where work hours are defined differently than these default durations. Zero isn't a valid quantity for hours or days.
Project for the Web uses these durations (whether default or customized) to convert to hours only when you define effort on a task.

Unassigned tasks use the project's calendar for scheduling. However, when you initially assign a resource, the scheduling engine updates the task to respect the resource's calendar. Subsequent changes to a task with an assignment follow the scheduling mode of the project. To learn more about the influence of calendars on tasks, see Resource Calendars in Project for the web and Task Start Times & Your Projects! .
Project for the Web doesn't respect business closures that you define in the resource or project calendars.

You can create a resource assignment only when the end of the resource's calendar rule overlaps the task's end date. Therefore, if your calendar rule ends before the end of the task, the assignment isn't scheduled, because the schedule doesn't have enough time for the resource to perform the task.

For example, a resource calendar begins on Wednesday, March 1, 2023, but ends on Friday, April 14, 2023. In the project where you add this resource, tasks that you assign to this resource can't end later than April 14, 2023. Otherwise, they aren't scheduled, because they exceed the last day of the resource's calendar.
The screenshot shows in green which tasks can be scheduled because they end before the last day of the resource availability, and in red, which ones aren't allowed.

sections.summary:
Here's a summary of the content in 3-5 clear, actionable sentences:

A task's duration in Project for the Web is calculated based on working hours between the start and finish dates, using default or customized duration units. When assigning a resource to a task, the task's schedule is updated to respect the resource's calendar, but subsequent changes follow the project's scheduling mode. Resource assignments can only be created when the resource's calendar overlaps with the task's end date, and tasks cannot be scheduled if they exceed the resource's available time. To ensure successful scheduling, ensure that the task's end date aligns with the resource's calendar availability.

sections.heading: Accessibility and keyboard shortcuts

blocks.text: The Schedule grid is fully accessible and works with screen readers such as Narrator, JAWS, or NVDA. You can move through the grid area by using arrow keys (as in Microsoft Excel). Use the Tab key to advance through the interactive user interface elements. Use the Down arrow key, the Enter key, or the Spacebar to select and open the drop-down menus.

sections.summary: The Schedule grid is fully accessible and compatible with popular screen readers. To navigate the grid, use arrow keys to move around, similar to Microsoft Excel. Press the Tab key to advance through interactive elements, and use the Down arrow, Enter, or Spacebar to select and open drop-down menus. This allows users to easily access and interact with the grid using their keyboard.

sections.heading: Planning a project in s s

blocks.text: You can also use s s and plan your project based on agile project management. For more information, see Plan a project in s s in Project for the web .

sections.summary: You can plan a project in s s using agile project management principles. This approach allows for flexibility and adaptability in your project planning. For more information on how to do this, refer to the guide on planning a project in s s in Project for the web.

input_title: Work breakdown structures overview | Microsoft Learn
page_title: Work breakdown structures overview | Microsoft Learn
meta_description: Learn about using work breakdown structures (WBS).

sections.heading: Share via

blocks.text: Access to this page requires authorization. You can try signing in or changing directories .
Access to this page requires authorization. You can try changing directories .

sections.summary: You don't have permission to access this page. To resolve this, try signing in with your account credentials. If you're already signed in, try changing directories to see if that resolves the issue. If you still can't access the page, you may need to contact the administrator for further assistance.

sections.heading: In this article

blocks.text: A work breakdown structure (WBS) is a description of the work that you do for a project. It's a hierarchy of tasks that represents the project team's understanding of the composition of work, and of the size, cost, and duration of each component or task. A WBS has three major purposes:

items: Describe the breakdown or composition of work in tasks.
Schedule the project work.
Estimate the cost of each task.

blocks.text: The degree of detail in a WBS depends on the level of accuracy that you require in estimates and the level of tracking that you need against those estimates. Projects that have low tolerance for slippages in schedule or cost usually require a more detailed WBS, and diligent tracking of work progress and cost against the WBS. This kind of project is common in the construction and engineering industries.

By contrast, projects in industries such as media and advertising, software, and IT infrastructure tend to be one of a kind, and productivity is relative to the experience and competency of the individual who is performing the task. Therefore, these industries use a WBS to get an approximation of the size of a project, not to track the progress of that project in detail.

Building a WBS is an intensive process that is done over a long period, and that requires collaboration and information from a wide variety of people. This article describes how you can use WBS enhancements to meet your requirements for estimates and tracking.

sections.summary: A work breakdown structure (WBS) is a hierarchical description of project tasks that helps teams understand the composition, size, cost, and duration of each task. A WBS serves three main purposes: breaking down work into tasks, scheduling project work, and estimating task costs. The level of detail in a WBS depends on the project's requirements, with high-risk projects (e.g., construction, engineering) needing more detailed WBS and tracking, while one-of-a-kind projects (e.g., media, software) use WBS for rough project sizing. Building a WBS is a collaborative and time-consuming process that requires input from various stakeholders.

sections.heading: Prerequisites for creating a WBS

blocks.text: To create a WBS, you must be able to create a work schedule and estimate the cost of work.

sections.summary: To create a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS), two key prerequisites must be met. First, you need to be able to create a work schedule that outlines the tasks and timeline. Additionally, you must be able to estimate the cost of the work involved. Meeting these prerequisites will enable you to effectively break down the project into manageable tasks and create a comprehensive WBS.

sections.heading: Prerequisites for creating a work schedule

blocks.text: To use the full scheduling capabilities of the WBS features, complete the following setup steps:

items: Set up a default calendar and a project calendar: Select Project management and accounting > Setup > Project management and accounting parameters > Scheduling . In the Default working calendar field, specify a default calendar. This calendar is the default working calendar for any new project that you create. Change the default calendar for a specific project. Select the project's details page, and then, on the Project team and scheduling FastTab, update the Scheduling calendar field by selecting another calendar.
Set up standard working days and working hours. The calendar that you set as the working calendar for your project is used in the WBS to determine the following information:

blocks.text: Set up a default calendar and a project calendar:

items: Select Project management and accounting > Setup > Project management and accounting parameters > Scheduling . In the Default working calendar field, specify a default calendar. This calendar is the default working calendar for any new project that you create.

Change the default calendar for a specific project. Select the project's details page, and then, on the Project team and scheduling FastTab, update the Scheduling calendar field by selecting another calendar.

blocks.text: Set up standard working days and working hours. The calendar that you set as the working calendar for your project is used in the WBS to determine the following information:

items: Working days and holidays
The number of working hours in a day

sections.summary: To create a work schedule, set up a default calendar and a project calendar by going to Project management and accounting > Setup > Project management and accounting parameters > Scheduling. Specify a default calendar for new projects and update the Scheduling calendar field for specific projects if needed. Additionally, set up standard working days and working hours, as the working calendar determines working days, holidays, and daily working hours in the Work Breakdown Structure (WBS).

sections.heading: Prerequisites for estimating the cost of work

blocks.text: To use the full cost estimation capabilities of the WBS, set up the costs and sales prices for workers, categories of labor, expenses, and fees, and items.

items: To set up the cost and sales price of labor, expense, and fee categories, select Project management and accounting > Setup > Prices .
To set up the cost and sales price of items, use the Trade agreements page for each item on the Released products list page in Product information management.

sections.summary: To estimate the cost of work using the Work Breakdown Structure (WBS), you need to set up costs and sales prices for various components. This includes setting up costs and sales prices for labor, expenses, fees, and items. To do this, go to Project management and accounting > Setup > Prices for labor, expenses, and fees, and use the Trade agreements page for items on the Released products list page. By setting up these costs and prices, you can utilize the full cost estimation capabilities of the WBS.

sections.heading: Creating a WBS

blocks.text: Creating a WBS involves three activities:

items: Work decomposition â Create a breakdown of work into manageable chunks or tasks.
Work schedule â Estimate the time that is required to complete a task, set task interdependencies, and select the start and end dates for tasks.
Cost estimation â Estimate costs for each task.

blocks.text: The following sections discuss how the WBS capabilities can help with each of these activities.

sections.summary: To create a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS), you'll need to break down work into manageable tasks through work decomposition. Next, establish a work schedule by estimating task duration, setting interdependencies, and assigning start and end dates. Additionally, estimate the costs associated with each task. These steps help create a comprehensive WBS that outlines the scope of work and resources required.

sections.heading: Work decomposition

blocks.text: Creating a breakdown or decomposition of work is usually the first step in the process of creating a WBS. The WBS functionality supports the following basic constructs for work breakdown or decomposition.

sections.summary: To create a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS), start by decomposing work into smaller parts. This process involves breaking down a large project into manageable, smaller tasks. The WBS supports basic constructs for work decomposition, allowing you to organize and structure your project effectively. By doing so, you can create a clear and actionable plan for your project.

sections.heading: Project root task

blocks.text: The project root task is the top-level summary task for a project. You create all other project tasks beneath it. The name of the root task is always set to the project name. The effort, dates, and duration of the root node summarize the values for the tasks beneath the root task. You can't modify the properties of the root node or delete it.

sections.summary: The project root task is the top-level task in a project, serving as a summary of all other tasks. It's automatically named after the project and can't be modified or deleted. The root task's properties, such as effort, dates, and duration, are calculated based on the tasks beneath it. All other project tasks are created under the root task, making it the foundation of the project's task hierarchy.

sections.heading: Summary or container tasks

blocks.text: A summary task is a task that has sub-tasks or constituent tasks beneath it. A summary task doesn't have any work effort or cost of its own. Instead, the work effort and cost of a summary task are the sum of the work effort and cost of its constituent tasks. The earliest start date of the constituent tasks is used as the start date of the summary task, and the latest end date of the constituent tasks is used as the end date. You can modify the name of a summary task, but you can't modify the scheduling properties for effort, dates, and duration. If you delete a summary task, you also delete all its constituent tasks.

sections.summary: A summary task is a container that holds sub-tasks, but doesn't have its own work effort or cost. Its start and end dates are determined by the earliest start and latest end dates of its sub-tasks. You can rename a summary task, but its scheduling properties are automatically calculated and can't be changed. Deleting a summary task also deletes all its sub-tasks.

sections.heading: Leaf node tasks

blocks.text: A leaf node task represents the most granular work package on the project. A leaf node has an estimated effort, a planned number of resources, a planned start date and end date, and duration.
You can complete the following hierarchy operations to enable the creation of a work hierarchy or decomposition for a project.

sections.summary: A leaf node task is the smallest unit of work in a project, representing a specific task with a clear scope. It includes key details such as estimated effort, assigned resources, start and end dates, and duration. Leaf node tasks are the building blocks of a project's work hierarchy. By creating and organizing leaf node tasks, you can break down a large project into a manageable and structured work plan.

sections.heading: New task

blocks.text: You automatically add any new task that you create under the root node, and automatically assign a WBS number to the task. The WBS number represents the task's level in the hierarchy. For tasks in the first level under the project root task, the numbering scheme uses 1, 2, 3, and so on. For tasks that are under the first level, the numbering scheme uses 1.1, 1.2, 1.3, and so on. For each level that you add under a previous level, you add a new dotted series of numbers.
Currently, you can't customize the WBS numbering.

sections.summary: When creating a new task, it's automatically added under the root node and assigned a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) number. The WBS number indicates the task's level in the hierarchy, with the first level using numbers 1, 2, 3, and so on. Subsequent levels use a dotted numbering scheme, such as 1.1, 1.2, 1.3, and add a new series of numbers for each level. This numbering scheme is automatic and currently cannot be customized.

sections.heading: Indent task

blocks.text: When you indent a task, it becomes a child of the task that precedes it. The WBS number of the new child task is automatically recalculated based on the WBS number of its new parent. The parent task is now a summary or container task, and therefore becomes a roll-up of its constituent tasks.

When you indent tasks under a task that was a leaf node before the indent operation, the newly created summary task loses its own dates, effort, and number of resources. It now uses a summary of the values of its new constituent tasks.

sections.summary: Indenting a task makes it a child of the preceding task, automatically updating its WBS number. The parent task becomes a summary task, rolling up the values of its child tasks. When a leaf node task is indented, it loses its original dates, effort, and resource allocation, instead using a summary of its child tasks' values. This change helps to create a hierarchical structure and simplify task management.

sections.heading: Outdent task

blocks.text: When you outdent a task, it's no longer a constituent task of its parent. The WBS number of this task is automatically recalculated to reflect the task's new level in the hierarchy. The effort, cost, and dates of the task's previous parent task are recalculated to exclude that task.

sections.summary: When you outdent a task, it is removed from its parent task and becomes a separate entity. The task's WBS number is automatically updated to reflect its new position in the hierarchy. As a result, the effort, cost, and dates of the original parent task are recalculated to exclude the outdented task. This change helps to reorganize and refine your task structure.

sections.heading: Move up and Move down

blocks.text: When you select Move up and Move down , you change the position of a task within its parent's hierarchy. The position of a task doesn't affect the task's effort, cost, dates, or duration. However, the WBS number of the task is automatically recalculated to reflect the task's new position.

sections.summary: Using "Move up" and "Move down" changes a task's position within its parent's hierarchy. This action does not affect the task's effort, cost, dates, or duration. The task's WBS number is automatically updated to reflect its new position. This feature allows for easy reorganization of tasks without altering their core attributes.

sections.heading: Schedule estimation

blocks.text: Schedule estimation is usually the second step in creating a WBS. As a best practice, complete schedule estimation after you create the tasks. The Work breakdown structure page in Finance has two sections. The upper pane is intended for schedule estimation, and the lower pane includes an Estimated costs and revenues tab that you can use for cost estimation.

sections.summary: To create a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS), schedule estimation is typically the second step. It's best to complete schedule estimation after defining tasks. The WBS page has two sections: the upper pane for schedule estimation and the lower pane for cost estimation. Use these sections to plan and organize your project timeline and budget.

sections.heading: Task dependencies

blocks.text: In a WBS, you can create a predecessor relationship between tasks. When you assign predecessor tasks to a task, that task can start only after all its predecessor tasks are completed. The planned start date of the task is automatically set to the latest date of all its predecessors.

sections.summary: In a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS), tasks can be linked through predecessor relationships. This means a task can't start until all its predecessor tasks are completed. The start date of a task is automatically set to the latest completion date of its predecessors. This helps ensure tasks are done in the correct order and dependencies are managed effectively.

sections.heading: Task scheduling

blocks.text: The following factors determine the scheduling of leaf node tasks:

items: Predecessors
Effort
The number of resources
Start and end dates

blocks.text: The start date of a leaf node task that doesn't have predecessors is automatically set to the project's scheduling start date. The duration of a leaf node task is always calculated as the number of working days between its start and end dates.

sections.summary: When scheduling leaf node tasks, consider the following factors: predecessors, effort, resources, and start and end dates. If a leaf node task has no predecessors, its start date defaults to the project's scheduling start date. The task's duration is calculated based on the number of working days between its start and end dates. This ensures accurate scheduling and resource allocation.

sections.heading: Scheduling rules

blocks.text: When you turn on automatic scheduling assistance, the following rules apply to task scheduling for leaf node tasks:

items: The project's scheduling calendar determines that the start and end dates of a task must be working days.
The latest end date of predecessor tasks automatically sets the start date of a task that has predecessors.
The effort for a task is automatically calculated as follows:

blocks.text: Number of people Ã Duration Ã Number of hours in a standard working day in the project calendar.

In some cases, you might want to deviate from these rules. You can turn off automatic scheduling to prevent Finance from automatically setting or correcting any properties of leaf node tasks. When you enter information for a task that causes a violation of any scheduling rules, a scheduling error icon is shown for the task. If you don't want scheduling errors to be displayed, select Scheduling errors are shown to turn off the feature.
The values for a summary or container task continue to be calculated as the sum of the values of the constituent tasks, regardless of whether automatic scheduling assistance is turned on or off.

sections.summary: When automatic scheduling assistance is turned on, tasks are scheduled based on the project calendar, predecessor tasks, and calculated effort. The effort is calculated by multiplying the number of people, duration, and standard working hours. You can turn off automatic scheduling to manually control task properties, but this may cause scheduling errors to appear. Scheduling errors can be hidden by selecting the "Scheduling errors are shown" option. Summary tasks will still calculate values based on constituent tasks, regardless of automatic scheduling status.

sections.heading: Fixing scheduling errors

blocks.text: When automatic scheduling assistance is turned on, scheduling errors aren't likely to occur. However, if you turn off automatic scheduling assistance and then turn it back on later, scheduling error icons might appear in the WBS.

sections.summary: To minimize scheduling errors, keep automatic scheduling assistance turned on. If you turn it off and then back on, errors may occur. In this case, scheduling error icons will appear in the Work Breakdown Structure (WBS). To resolve the issue, review and correct the errors indicated by the icons.

sections.heading: Fixing scheduling errors by task

blocks.text: When you double-click the schedule error icon for a specific task, a dialog box displays all the scheduling errors for that task. You can decide which scheduling errors to fix for the task.

sections.summary: To fix scheduling errors for a specific task, double-click the schedule error icon. A dialog box will appear, listing all scheduling errors for that task. Review the errors and select which ones to correct. This allows you to address and resolve scheduling issues for individual tasks.

sections.heading: Fixing all scheduling errors

blocks.text: If you want Finance to fix all scheduling errors in the WBS, on the Action Pane, select Fix all scheduling discrepancies .
This feature can cause significant modifications to the WBS. Errors are corrected in the following order:

items: The estimated effort on all tasks is modified so that it's equal to the capacity that is defined in the project calendar.

blocks.text: The estimated effort on all tasks is modified so that it's equal to the capacity that is defined in the project calendar.

items: The start date of each task is modified to remove gaps in the start dates of predecessor tasks.

sections.summary: To fix all scheduling errors in the Work Breakdown Structure (WBS), select "Fix all scheduling discrepancies" on the Action Pane. This feature corrects errors by modifying task effort to match the project calendar's capacity. It also adjusts task start dates to eliminate gaps between predecessor tasks. Note that this feature can make significant changes to the WBS.

sections.heading: Cost estimation

blocks.text: Enter the cost estimation for each leaf node task by using the Estimated costs and revenues tab in the lower pane of the Work breakdown structure page.

You can't modify the cost estimation for a summary or container task. The cost estimation for a summary task equals the sum of the cost estimation of its leaf node tasks. The estimated total cost for each task is calculated as the sum of the estimated cost amounts for the following transaction types:

items: Labor
Item or material
Expenses

blocks.text: Use a Fee transaction type to estimate fee-based revenue. This transaction type doesn't have a cost component and therefore isn't considered when costs are estimated.
Use an On-account transaction type to record the contracted sales value in a fixed-value type of project. This transaction type also isn't considered when costs are estimated.
When you estimate costs for labor, material, and expenses for each task, you must assign a project category to the estimated cost.

sections.summary: To estimate costs, enter values for each leaf node task in the Work breakdown structure page. The estimated total cost is calculated by summing labor, item/material, and expense costs for each task. Assign a project category to each estimated cost for labor, material, and expenses. Note that summary tasks' costs are automatically calculated as the sum of their leaf node tasks' costs, and certain transaction types (Fee, On-account) are not included in cost estimations.

sections.heading: Estimating labor costs

blocks.text: For each leaf node task, assign a work effort in hours and a default category. When you set up a schedule for a task, the labor cost estimate for that task is automatically added in the default category for labor. This cost estimate is displayed on the Estimated costs and revenue tab in the Line details grid for that task. If you require more labor cost estimates, add them on this tab. If you increase or decrease hours on the labor cost estimate, the schedule for the task is automatically recalculated.

sections.summary: To estimate labor costs, assign a work effort in hours and a default category to each task. The labor cost estimate is automatically added to the default category and displayed in the Estimated costs and revenue tab. You can add additional labor cost estimates as needed on this tab. Changes to the labor cost estimate hours will automatically recalculate the task schedule.

sections.heading: Estimating expense and material costs

blocks.text: The Estimated costs and revenue tab also lets you estimate expense and material costs for a task, if you require estimates.

The cost and sales price for each labor or expense estimate line are based on the setup that you define for each category in the pricing tables at Project management and accounting > Setup > Pricing . For items, cost and sales price are added by default from the item or trade agreements on the Released products list page in Product information management.

sections.summary: To estimate expense and material costs for a task, use the Estimated costs and revenue tab. The costs and sales prices for labor and expenses are based on the pricing tables set up in Project management and accounting. For items, the costs and sales prices are automatically added from the Released products list page in Product information management. This setup allows for accurate cost estimation and helps with project budgeting.

sections.heading: Tracking progress on the WBS

blocks.text: Some industries track the progress of a project against a WBS at a granular level, whereas others track progress at a higher level of the WBS. This section describes how you can use WBS tracking for your project requirements.
Finance has three views for the WBS of a project: the Planning view, Effort tracking view, and Cost tracking view.

sections.summary: To track progress on a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS), you can choose the level of detail that suits your project needs. Some industries track progress at a granular level, while others prefer a higher-level view. In finance, three views are available for WBS tracking: Planning, Effort tracking, and Cost tracking. These views allow you to monitor project progress from different perspectives.

sections.heading: Planning view

blocks.text: The Planning view displays the planned or baseline estimate of the schedule and cost information. Although the project WBS doesn't have features for tracking the version and baseline, the values in this view represent the baseline version. The Schedule estimation and Cost estimation sections of this article describe this view and how it's used to create a WBS.

sections.summary: The Planning view shows the original estimated schedule and cost of a project. It represents the baseline version of the project, even though the project's Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) doesn't have built-in version tracking. This view is used to create a WBS and is further explained in the Schedule estimation and Cost estimation sections. It provides a foundation for project planning and tracking.

sections.heading: Effort tracking view

blocks.text: The Effort tracking view displays the tracking of progress for tasks in the WBS. It compares the accumulated actual effort hours for a task to the planned effort hours. The following formulas provide the values in the Effort tracking view:

items: Progress percentage = Actual effort to date Ã· Planned effort for the task
Remaining effort (also known as Estimate-to-complete [ETC]) = Planned effort â Actual effort to date
Estimate at complete (EAC) = Remaining effort + Actual effort to date
Projected effort variance = Planned effort â EAC

blocks.text: The Effort tracking view displays a projection of the effort variance for the task, based on whether EAC is more or less than the planned effort:

items: If EAC is more than the planned effort, the task is projected to take more time than originally planned and is behind schedule.
If EAC is less than the planned effort, the task is projected to take less time than originally planned and is ahead of schedule.

sections.summary: The Effort tracking view monitors task progress in the Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) by comparing actual effort hours to planned hours. It calculates key metrics, including progress percentage, remaining effort, and estimated effort at completion. The view also projects effort variance, indicating if a task is ahead or behind schedule based on whether the estimated effort at completion is more or less than the planned effort. This allows for timely adjustments to ensure projects stay on track.

sections.heading: Project manager's re-projection of effort

blocks.text: Occasionally, the project manager or another persona that's tracking the progress of a project needs to revise the original estimates on a task. The task might be moving faster or slower than originally anticipated for various reasons. For example, the scope might be reduced, or workers might have less experience than originally planned. Projections are a project manager's perception of estimates, based on the current status on a project. In general, don't change the baseline numbers, because a project baseline represents a well-published document for the project's schedule and cost estimate that all stakeholders on the project agree to.
Project managers can modify the effort on tasks in two ways:

items: Modify the remaining effort to automatically update the actual remaining effort on the task.
Modify the progress percentage to automatically update the true progress on the task.

blocks.text: Both of these approaches cause a recalculation of the task's ETC, EAC, and progress percentage, and the projected effort variance on the task. The EAC, ETC, and progress percentage on summary tasks are also recalculated, and their projected effort variance is updated.

sections.summary: When a project's progress deviates from the original plan, the project manager can re-project the effort required to complete a task. This is done by modifying either the remaining effort or the progress percentage, which triggers a recalculation of the task's estimated time to completion (ETC), estimated cost at completion (EAC), and progress percentage. The original baseline numbers remain unchanged, as they represent a agreed-upon project schedule and cost estimate. The re-projection helps project managers adjust to changes in scope, worker experience, or other factors that impact task completion. This update also affects summary tasks, recalculating their EAC, ETC, and progress percentage.

sections.heading: Modified effort on summary tasks

blocks.text: You can modify the effort on summary or container tasks. Regardless of whether you modify these values by using the remaining effort or the progress percentage on the summary tasks, the calculations occur automatically in the following order:

items: The EAC, ETC, and progress percentage on the task are calculated.
The new EAC is distributed to the child tasks in the same proportion as the original EAC amount.
The new EAC on each leaf node task is calculated.
The remaining effort and progress percentage are recalculated for all the affected child tasks, based on the new EAC value. The effort variance of the tasks is also recalculated.
The EAC of the summary tasks is also recalculated from the leaf nodes.

blocks.text: Select Expand to level in the Effort tracking view to set the level at which to track and maintain your WBS. The WBS automatically expands to that level in the Effort tracking view whenever you open it.

sections.summary: Modifying the effort on summary tasks triggers automatic calculations for EAC, ETC, and progress percentage. The new EAC is distributed proportionally to child tasks, and their remaining effort and progress percentage are recalculated. This also updates the effort variance and recalculates the EAC of summary tasks from leaf nodes. To control the level of detail, use the "Expand to level" feature in the Effort tracking view to set the desired WBS level.

sections.heading: Cost tracking view

blocks.text: The Cost tracking view tracks cost consumption for a task. It compares the actual cost spent on a task to date with the planned cost for the task. The following formulas provide the values in the Cost tracking view:

items: Percentage of cost consumed = Actual cost to date Ã· Planned cost for the task
Cost to complete (CTC) = Planned cost â Actual cost to date
Estimate at complete (EAC) = CTC + Actual cost to date
Projected cost variance = Planned cost â EAC

blocks.text: The Cost tracking view shows a projection of the cost variance for the task, based on whether EAC is more or less than the planned cost:

items: If EAC is more than the planned cost, the task uses more money than originally planned and is over budget.
If EAC is less than the planned cost, the task uses less money than originally planned and is under budget.

sections.summary: The Cost tracking view monitors task expenses by comparing actual costs to planned costs. It calculates key metrics, including the percentage of cost consumed, cost to complete, and estimated cost at completion. The view also projects cost variance, indicating if a task is over or under budget based on whether the estimated cost exceeds or falls short of the planned cost. This helps identify potential budget issues and inform financial decisions.

sections.heading: Project manager's re-projection of cost

blocks.text: Project managers use CTC to revise the original cost estimate on a task. The project manager can modify the CTC value to the cost that is required to complete the task. If you modify the CTC value, the task's CTC, EAC, percentage of cost consumed, projected cost variance, and the task are recalculated. The EAC, ETC, and percentage of cost consumed on the summary tasks are also recalculated, and their projected cost variance is updated.

When you revise effort for a WBS task in the Effort tracking view, the task's CTC, EAC, percentage of cost consumed, and projected cost variance are all recalculated in the Cost tracking view. However, cost revisions don't affect the values in the Effort tracking view, because the cost by transaction type (labor, material, or expense) or project category isn't revised.

sections.summary:
Here's a summary of the project manager's re-projection of cost:

Project managers can revise the original cost estimate of a task by modifying the CTC (Cost to Complete) value. This change recalculates the task's CTC, EAC (Estimate at Completion), percentage of cost consumed, and projected cost variance. The revision also affects the summary tasks, updating their EAC, ETC (Estimate to Complete), percentage of cost consumed, and projected cost variance. Revising effort for a task in the Effort tracking view will also trigger these recalculations in the Cost tracking view.

sections.heading: Projection revision for costs on summary tasks

blocks.text: You can revise costs on summary tasks, and the calculations occur automatically in the following order:

items: The EAC, CTC, and percentage of cost consumed on the task are recalculated.
The new EAC is distributed to the child tasks in the same proportion as the original EAC on the tasks.
The new EAC for each task is calculated.
The CTS and percentage of cost consumed are recalculated for the affected child tasks, based on the EAC value. The cost variance of the tasks is also recalculated.
The EAC for all the summary tasks is recalculated based on this change.

blocks.text: Select Expand to level in the Cost tracking view to set the level at which to track and maintain your WBS. The WBS expands to that level in the Cost tracking view whenever you open it.

sections.summary: Revising costs on summary tasks triggers automatic calculations, updating the Estimated at Completion (EAC), Cost to Complete (CTC), and percentage of cost consumed. The new EAC is then distributed to child tasks proportionally, and their EAC, Cost to Schedule (CTS), and cost variance are recalculated. This change also triggers a recalculation of the EAC for all summary tasks. To control the level of detail, use the "Expand to level" feature in the Cost tracking view to set the desired level for your Work Breakdown Structure (WBS).

sections.heading: Earned value management

blocks.text: Use the earned value method (EVM) to track the progress of a project. You can view earned value metrics on the project managerâs Role Center. The earned value chart component shows the time-phased values of planned value and actual cost. Earned value as of the current date is shown as a point. Time-phased data for earned value isn't currently available.
The time phase on the earned value chart is displayed by week or by month. This section describes the three pillars of EVM: planned value, earned value, and actual cost.

sections.summary: To track project progress, use the Earned Value Method (EVM), which can be accessed through the project manager's Role Center. The Earned Value chart displays planned value and actual cost over time, with the current earned value shown as a point. The chart can be viewed by week or month, but time-phased earned value data is not currently available. EVM is based on three key metrics: planned value, earned value, and actual cost.

sections.heading: Planned value

blocks.text: EVM theory states that the planned value plot represents the rate at which the project's team planned to earn value on the project.

Finance uses the 0:100 earning rule when it plots planned value. Under this rule, the value of the task is posted to the task as of its end date. No value is posted until the task is 100 percent completed.

In Project management and accounting, you enter the end date of the leaf nodes and the planned cost for it. When the graph of planned value is displayed by week, planned value is summarized by week for all leaf node tasks during the project.

sections.summary: The planned value plot represents the rate at which a project team planned to earn value. According to the 0:100 earning rule, task value is only posted when the task is 100% complete, at its end date. To create a planned value graph, enter the end date and planned cost for each task, and the system will summarize the planned value by week for all tasks. This allows for a visual representation of the project's planned progress over time.

sections.heading: Earned value

blocks.text: EVM theory states that the earned value plot represents the rate at which the project's team is actually earning value on the project.

Finance uses the 0:100 earning rule when it plots earned value. Under this rule, the value of the task is posted to the task as of its end date. No value is posted until the task is 100 percent completed.

When you calculate earned value, consider the progress percentage of each task. Under the 0:100 earning rule, only tasks that are completed in a given period are considered for the calculation of earned value as of the end of that period. The graph calculates earned value on the project for all tasks that are completed.

Currently, the system for WBS tracking doesn't have data structures to store historic progress percentages on each task. Therefore, you can report earned value only as of the time that the cube is processed. Process the cube regularly to update the earned value data that is shown on the Role Center.

sections.summary:
Here is a summary of the section on Earned Value:

Earned value represents the rate at which a project team is earning value, calculated based on task progress. The 0:100 earning rule states that a task's value is only posted when it is 100% complete. To calculate earned value, consider the progress percentage of each task, but only include tasks that are fully completed in a given period. Due to system limitations, earned value can only be reported as of the time the data is processed, so regular cube processing is necessary to update the data.

sections.heading: Actual cost

blocks.text: EVM theory states that the actual cost plot represents the rate at which money is being spent on the project.
Use transactions that you post to a project to plot the actual cost line. Summarize the costs by date. Use this data to graph the actual costs by week or by month on the earned value chart.

sections.summary: To track actual costs in a project, use posted transactions to plot the actual cost line. This line represents the rate at which money is being spent. Summarize costs by date and graph them by week or month on the earned value chart. This visual representation helps monitor project expenses over time.

sections.heading: Schedule variance

blocks.text: During planning, you create a forecast for work on a timeline. Therefore, planned value is the rate at which project planners thought work would be completed on the project. After a project is in progress, work is completed, and the project earns value. By comparing planned value to earned value, you can view how work on a project is progressing. The result of this comparison is called schedule variance.

If the planned value for a period is more than the earned value, the amount of work that the project team completed is less than what was planned. Therefore, the project is behind schedule. Because planned value and earned value are expressed in monetary terms, the lag time on the project also has a monetary value.

If the planned value for a period is less than the earned value, the amount of work that the project team completed is more than what was planned. Therefore, the project is ahead of schedule. This lead time also has a monetary value.

sections.summary: Schedule variance measures a project's progress by comparing planned value (the forecasted rate of work completion) to earned value (the actual work completed). If earned value is less than planned value, the project is behind schedule, with a monetary value assigned to the lag time. Conversely, if earned value exceeds planned value, the project is ahead of schedule, with a monetary value assigned to the lead time. This comparison helps project managers assess progress and make informed decisions to get the project back on track.

sections.heading: Cost variance

blocks.text: Because earned value uses the cost price as the multiplier, earned value indicates the cost of the work that the project team completes on a project. As a project progresses, the transaction log provides a record of money that the project team actually spends on that project. By comparing earned value to actual cost, you can view the amount of money that the project team is spending versus the value that the project team earns. The result of this comparison is called cost variance.
If the actual cost that the project team spends for a period is more than the earned value, the project team spent more money than it earned. Therefore, the project is over budget.
If the actual cost that the project team spends for a period is less than the earned value, the project team earned more money than it spent. Therefore, the project is under budget.

sections.summary: Cost variance measures the difference between the actual cost of a project and its earned value. To calculate cost variance, compare the actual cost spent on a project to its earned value, which represents the value of work completed. If actual cost exceeds earned value, the project is over budget, while a lower actual cost indicates the project is under budget. This comparison helps project teams track their spending and stay on budget.

sections.heading: WBS templates

blocks.text: Use the WBS templates functionality to create standard templates for projects. If the projects that your company offers involve many repeatable work, consider creating a WBS template.

Create a WBS template from the WBS of an existing project, so that you can reuse knowledge and best practices that you gathered during the planning of that project on similar projects in the future. However, sometimes, it might not make sense to save the whole WBS as a template. Therefore, you can also create templates from parts of the WBS for a project.

sections.summary: To streamline project planning, use WBS templates to create standard templates for repeatable projects. Create a template from an existing project's WBS to reuse knowledge and best practices on similar projects. You can also create templates from specific parts of a WBS, rather than the entire thing, for more flexibility. This helps to save time and ensure consistency across projects.

sections.heading: Saving a projectâs WBS as a template

blocks.text: After you create a template, import it into a new projectâs WBS under the root node, or under any task in the projectâs WBS.

sections.summary: To reuse a project's Work Breakdown Structure (WBS), save it as a template. This template can then be imported into a new project's WBS. You can import the template under the root node or under any specific task in the new project's WBS. This allows for efficient reuse of existing WBS structures in new projects.

sections.heading: Import a WBS template into a project's WBS

blocks.text: When you import tasks, the template organizes the tasks by the start date of the task that you import under. During import, predecessor relationships on the template tasks help compute the start dates for the imported tasks. The destination project's standard work calendar helps compute the end dates of the imported tasks, so the working days and standard working hours that you define in the current project's work calendar stay the same.
Cost amounts and sales prices on the estimate lines make sure that prices specific to the project or project contract have valid dates.

sections.summary: When importing a WBS template into a project, tasks are organized by start date. The template's predecessor relationships help determine the start dates of imported tasks, while the project's standard work calendar determines the end dates. This ensures that the project's working days and hours are preserved. Additionally, cost amounts and sales prices are updated to reflect valid dates specific to the project or contract.

sections.heading: Differences between a projectâs WBS and a WBS template

items: The tasks in WBS templates don't have start dates or end dates.
The working and non-working days aren't set for WBS templates.
WBS templates always use the scheduling calendar that is set up as the default calendar for all projects.
The default scheduling calendar is used only to find out hours in a standard working day.
Predecessor relationships aren't copied to a WBS template.
Because WBS templates don't have dates, the start date logic that is based on a predecessor's end date isn't required.
A labor cost estimate line is automatically created when a task is created in a WBS template. The sales price and cost amount are copied from the selected worker.
Expense and item costs can be created manually, just as they can on a project's WBS.
Scheduling errors are displayed when there are deviations from the following formula:

blocks.text: The tasks in WBS templates don't have start dates or end dates.
The working and non-working days aren't set for WBS templates.
WBS templates always use the scheduling calendar that is set up as the default calendar for all projects.
The default scheduling calendar is used only to find out hours in a standard working day.
Predecessor relationships aren't copied to a WBS template.
Because WBS templates don't have dates, the start date logic that is based on a predecessor's end date isn't required.
A labor cost estimate line is automatically created when a task is created in a WBS template. The sales price and cost amount are copied from the selected worker.
Expense and item costs can be created manually, just as they can on a project's WBS.
Scheduling errors are displayed when there are deviations from the following formula:
Effort = Number of resources Ã Duration Ã Number of hours in a standard working day

items: You can correct all scheduling errors at the same time by clicking Fix all scheduling errors .
Alternatively, you can correct scheduling errors individually by clicking the warning icon for each task.

blocks.text: You can correct all scheduling errors at the same time by clicking Fix all scheduling errors .
Alternatively, you can correct scheduling errors individually by clicking the warning icon for each task.

sections.summary: A project's Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) and a WBS template have key differences. Unlike a project's WBS, a WBS template doesn't include start or end dates, working or non-working days, or predecessor relationships. When creating tasks in a WBS template, labor cost estimates are automatically generated based on the selected worker's rates. Scheduling errors in a WBS template can be corrected individually or all at once if they deviate from the formula: Effort = Number of resources × Duration × Number of hours in a standard working day.
